lesson.menu_desc: Phase 2 -- reported questions, for retelling what people asked -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 16 -- Said and Done -- Reported Questions
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Mark Kazuyoshi Seki Omagari | Aula 16 -- Said and Done

listenings.text: Hi Mark. Last class we talked about the people who make things work, and you described them with relative clauses. Today we learn how to retell the questions those people ask, and for that English uses reported questions. Here is the key idea. A direct question, like where do you live, becomes a reported question when we tell someone about it later. Three things change. First, the word order goes back to normal, so we say she asked where I lived, not she asked where did I live. Second, we drop the auxiliary do, does or did, so what does he want becomes she asked what he wanted. Third, the verb usually shifts one step back into the past, so are you ready becomes she asked if I was ready. For a yes or no question, we add if or whether, and for a wh-question we keep the question word. And there is no question mark at the end, because a reported question is really a statement. Listen for she asked where, he wondered whether, and they inquired if. So this week, think about the last interview or interesting chat you saw, and try to report three questions that were asked. See you in class, Mark.

Let me tell you about an interview I watched with a game designer, because the questions were really clever. First, the host asked how the studio had started, and the designer explained the whole story. Then she inquired whether the team had expected the game to be so popular, and he laughed and said no. She also wondered what the hardest part of the project was, and he pointed out that the testing stage had taken months. At one point she probed why they had chosen such an unusual art style, and he replied that they wanted to stand out. Near the end, a fan in the audience raised a question and asked if there would be a sequel, but the designer would not confirm anything. The host even requested that he reveal one secret, and he refused with a smile. What I liked most was that every answer led to a new question, so the conversation never felt flat. I kept thinking about how I would respond if someone asked me the same things.

extra_audio.text: First, you start with a reporting verb, like she asked, he wondered or they inquired. Then, for a yes or no question you add if or whether, and for a wh-question you keep the question word, such as where, what or why. After that, you put the subject and the verb in normal statement order, with no inversion. Next, you remove the auxiliary do, does or did, because a reported question is not a real question. Finally, you usually shift the verb one step back into the past, and you never add a question mark at the end.
